Welcome back to Dry Powder. I\xe2\x80\x99m Bill Cohan. If Warner Bros. Discovery\xe2\x80\x99s fourth quarter 2024 performance is a harbinger of things to come, the worst may be over for David Zaslav. On Thursday, the company\xe2\x80\x99s direct-to-consumer unit reported adjusted EBITDA of $677 million for 2024, up exponentially from $103 million the year before, plus strong performance from the Warners studio segment.

Mar 02, 2025

William D. Cohan

Welcome back to Dry Powder. I\xe2\x80\x99m Bill Cohan .

If Warner Bros. Discovery\xe2\x80\x99s fourth quarter 2024 performance is a harbinger of things to come, the worst may be over for David Zaslav . On Thursday, the company\xe2\x80\x99s direct-to-consumer unit reported adjusted EBITDA of $677 million for 2024, up exponentially from $103 million the year before, plus strong performance from the Warners studio segment. Then, of course, there\xe2\x80\x99s the $19 billion that Zaz and C.F.O. Gunnar Wiedenfels have managed to shave off the company\xe2\x80\x99s debt load in less than three years. Investor sentiment seems to be responding: In the past six months, the stock is up 51 percent, even though it\xe2\x80\x99s still down 55 percent since the company was formed. So is the glass half empty or half full? More on all that below.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

  • The Trump Media dip : Equity investors in Trump Media & Technology Group, Donald Trump \xe2\x80\x99s folly of a publicly traded company, finally seem to be getting the message that there\xe2\x80\x99s no there there. Last month, TMTG reported revenue of just $3.6 million in 2024 and a net loss of some $400 million \xe2\x80\x94almost all of which is attributable to the company\xe2\x80\x99s sole enterprise, Truth Social. During the past month, more or less tracking with the start of Trump II, the company\xe2\x80\x99s stock is down 25 percent while the S&P 500 is down only 2.5 percent. (This is not investment advice.)

    In December, Trump transferred his 114.75 million shares in DJT to a revocable trust, of which he is the sole beneficiary. Miraculously, those shares are still worth around $2.7 billion these days. But some degree of reality seems to be breaking out all over Trump\xe2\x80\x99s financial toys. In addition to the decline in DJT, the price of $TRUMP\xe2\x80\x94the Donald Trump memecoin that his S.E.C. recently ruled was not a security and therefore free from regulation\xe2\x80\x94is down 58 percent in the past month. $MELANIA, the equally ridiculous Melania Trump memecoin, is also down 64 percent. Could a modicum of sanity be kicking in?

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR

PERFORMANCE UNLEASHED
With a distinct sporting personality, the Range Rover Sport is a peerless performer.
EXPLORE

Meanwhile, across the Atlantic\xe2\x80\xa6

Marion Maneker
  • A Brave Neuendorf World : The long-running battle for control of Artnet, the art media and auction data company, finally came to a head yesterday during the annual general meeting in Berlin, which lasted 14 hours and caused the stock to drop by 20 percent. At the shareholder event, grievances were aired, tables were pounded, and one shareholder even complained the company wasn\xe2\x80\x99t doing enough to protect him from the Spanish aristocrat sitting beside him. The aristocrat happened to be the husband of prospective board member Sophie Neuendorf , who was, he claimed, trying to intimidate him. One person in attendance told me that the meeting resembled a \xe2\x80\x9c Jerry Springer episode at a circus.\xe2\x80\x9d

    The annual general meeting had attracted a number of factions\xe2\x80\x94some existing shareholders, others waiting in the wings\xe2\x80\x94interested in reviving the company to cater to a perceived underserved demand for art market information. Yet, according to one interested party in attendance, no one seemed to have an overall strategy for the company, nor did anyone present a clear plan for its future. Artnet C.E.O. Jacob Pabst presided over the event. His sister Sophie was a candidate for a board seat, and his brother Albert was seen working the room to drum up support for the family\xe2\x80\x99s slate. Hans Neuendorf , the 88-year-old founder of Artnet and paterfamilias of the Neuendorf clan, whose members take substantial salaries from the company, was notably absent.

    That was probably a good thing. A significant part of the meeting, according to one neutral shareholder, consisted of investors airing their misgivings and\xc2\xa0 \xe2\x80\x9ctelling the board and management to resign today.\xe2\x80\x9d The result, according to this shareholder, was \xe2\x80\x9ctotal humiliation for the Neuendorfs\xe2\x80\x94all of the family members.\xe2\x80\x9d (Curiously, according to another shareholder, the shareholders excluded Hans from the list of board members they demanded to vacate their roles. Not that it really mattered.) [ Read More ]

Has Zaz Finally Seen His Shadow?

Three years after the fraught inception of David Zaslav\xe2\x80\x99s Warner Bros. Discovery, the company\xe2\x80\x99s Q4 earnings report suggests that things are finally turning a corner\xe2\x80\x94and investors seem to agree. But can Zaz continue to outrun the decline of the TV business?

William D. Cohan

Back in April 2022, Warner Bros. Discovery came into the world with a slew of near-existential disadvantages\xe2\x80\x94the company was burdened by a $55 billion debt load, a BBB credit rating, and a rocky operating environment for its array of linear TV offerings, cable channels, and Hollywood fare. The equity markets didn\xe2\x80\x99t look kindly upon the Frankenmonster that David Zaslav had created through Discovery Communications, his challenged business, and the vestiges of AT&T\xe2\x80\x99s Warner Media. Since the company\xe2\x80\x99s formation, the stock is down 55 percent. The S&P 500 has risen 20 percent during the same time period.

And yet if WBD\xe2\x80\x99s fourth quarter 2024 performance is a harbinger of things to come, the worst may be over. I\xe2\x80\x99ve long argued that WBD is really a publicly traded leveraged buyout, and the key to unlocking value was the simultaneous servicing of its debt\xe2\x80\x94an objective that Zaz has been highly incentivized to achieve\xe2\x80\x94in lockstep with the growth of its direct-to-consumer business and overall profitability. At some point, I\xe2\x80\x99ve contended, the company would pay down enough debt and make enough cash (and project the ability to rev up on both fronts) that investors would look past the ominous headlines about its declining cable assets, its forfeiture of the NBA, and challenges in the film division.

It looks like we\xe2\x80\x99ve now reached an inflection point. Net debt at WBD is at $34.6 billion\xe2\x80\x94$40 billion of gross debt, less $5.4 of cash and cash equivalents\xe2\x80\x94and the company\xe2\x80\x99s leverage ratio of 3.8x adjusted EBITDA is on its way to a ratio of 2.5x-3x gross leverage. Meanwhile, Max is growing, and Zaz has signaled harder than ever that he\xe2\x80\x99s willing to kill his darlings from Discovery. \xe2\x80\x9cD.T.C. net adds and EBITDA continue to shine,\xe2\x80\x9d Kutgun Maral , at Evercore ISI, wrote in a quick note after the earnings release on Thursday.

The $3 Billion Improvement

Indeed, WBD has paid down $19 billion of its debt load in nearly three years. \xe2\x80\x9cWe will continue to be super, super focused on this,\xe2\x80\x9d C.F.O. Gunnar Wiedenfels said during the company\xe2\x80\x99s earnings call on Thursday morning. As I\xe2\x80\x99ve reported before, the tenor of the WBD debt is also quite advantageous to the company, at least in the current interest-rate environment. At the end of December, the average maturity of the company\xe2\x80\x99s remaining debt was 13.4 years, at an average interest rate cost of 4.7 percent. That\xe2\x80\x99s tasty for a company with a BBB credit rating, considering the yield on the 10-year Treasury is currently 4.2 percent.

WBD does have some near-term debt maturities, including $2.2 billion due in March, which explains the company\xe2\x80\x99s logic for carrying $5.4 billion in cash on its balance sheet. On the earnings call, Gunnar said the balance sheet is in \xe2\x80\x9cvery good shape now\xe2\x80\x9d and that he is not worried about any near-term debt maturities. \xe2\x80\x9cI view our capital structure as a real asset,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. I don\xe2\x80\x99t know when, or if, the credit rating agencies will upgrade WBD\xe2\x80\x99s BBB credit rating. But when they do\xe2\x80\x94and it should be soon(ish), I would think\xe2\x80\x94that will be the day the rocket fuel gets injected into the WBD publicly traded L.B.O. (This is not investment advice.)

This isn\xe2\x80\x99t just a balance sheet narrative, however. WBD has achieved a fair amount of success in the fastest growing parts of the business. Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s strategy of internationalizing Max, WBD\xe2\x80\x99s direct-to-consumer offering, seems to be paying off. Total D.T.C. subscribers reached 117 million in the fourth quarter, with the majority of those subs, nearly 60 million, coming from outside the United States. After penetrating Latin America, the service now has toeholds in the U.K., Ireland, Australia, Germany, and Italy. \xe2\x80\x9cMax became one of the very few global scaled and profitable streaming services,\xe2\x80\x9d the company wrote in a letter to shareholders that accompanied the earnings report.

There was also unexpected subscriber growth in the U.S., with some 4.5 million new subscribers domestically, as compared to analysts expectations of 2.8 million. \xe2\x80\x9cThe U.S. is a pretty mature market,\xe2\x80\x9d Zaz said. \xe2\x80\x9cWe think that there\xe2\x80\x99s still some growth, given the quality of our service, and we\xe2\x80\x99re fighting for that.\xe2\x80\x9d Meanwhile, streaming is now solidly profitable at WBD. In the fourth quarter, D.T.C. adjusted EBITDA\xe2\x80\x94I hate that term, as Zaz knows\xe2\x80\x94was $409 million, up from a loss in the fourth quarter of 2023 and above the $275 million that Wall Street was expecting, as Maral wrote in his note. For all of 2024, D.T.C.\xe2\x80\x99s adjusted EBITDA was $677 million, up exponentially from $103 million in 2023.

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR

PERFORMANCE UNLEASHED
With a distinct sporting personality, the Range Rover Sport is a peerless performer.
EXPLORE

Zaz wasn\xe2\x80\x99t shy about touting the success of this division. \xe2\x80\x9cOur direct-to-consumer business contributed almost $700 million in EBITDA, a $3 billion improvement in just two years,\xe2\x80\x9d he said on the call. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd we expect direct-to-consumer EBITDA to nearly double in 2025.\xe2\x80\x9d He specifically cited the success globally of The White Lotus , House of the Dragon , and The Pitt as \xe2\x80\x9ccompelling,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cquality,\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9coriginal storytelling.\xe2\x80\x9d ( The Pitt , of course, is not without its legal headaches, as my partner Eriq Gardner can assure you .) Maral wrote in a subsequent note on Friday that WBD\xe2\x80\x99s D.T.C. \xe2\x80\x9ctrends\xe2\x80\x9d remain \xe2\x80\x9chighly encouraging,\xe2\x80\x9d with subscribers growing to \xe2\x80\x9cover\xe2\x80\x9d 150 million by the end of next year and EBITDA \xe2\x80\x9cnearly\xe2\x80\x9d doubling to $1.3 billion this year.

The other happy story in the fourth quarter results came in WBD\xe2\x80\x99s studio segment. The company reported that the television production business manufactured 80 shows for both WBD and other streamers and broadcast networks, and was the top supplier of live-action TV to WBD and to others. Zaz highlighted hits such as The Penguin , \xe2\x80\x9cone of the top three most viewed original debut seasons on Max ever,\xe2\x80\x9d and Presumed Innocent , a big hit for Apple TV. In the fourth quarter, WBD\xe2\x80\x99s studio segment generated revenue of nearly $3.7 billion, up 16 percent year over year, and adjusted EBITDA\xe2\x80\x94kills me every time\xe2\x80\x94of $950 million, up a whopping 75 percent year over year. Zaz is especially jazzed about the debut of Warner Bros.\xe2\x80\x99 Superman film in July. ( Superman , alas, is also not without some legal issues , as Eriq can also remind you\xe2\x80\xa6)

The Cable Quagmire

WBD\xe2\x80\x99s linear TV offerings are still struggling, of course, no matter how creatively Robert Gibbs , the new WBD head of communications, is trying to apply lipstick to the pig. And, critically, this segment of WBD\xe2\x80\x99s business is still generating the bulk of the company\xe2\x80\x99s adjusted EBITDA. In 2024, the networks segment generated adjusted EBITDA of $8.1 billion, down 10 percent from the $9 billion in 2023. The trend lines are even worse: In the fourth quarter, adjusted EBITDA was $1.9 billion, down 13 percent from $2.2 billion from the year earlier quarter. \xe2\x80\x9cThe earnings generated by our linear networks continue to support our ability to invest in our transformation,\xe2\x80\x9d the company wrote to shareholders. You\xe2\x80\x99ve got to hand it to Gibbs for his spin capabilities.

That, of course, is why Zaz recently reorged WBD to make it easier to potentially spin off CNN, TNT, the Food Network, and other declining cable assets into their own publicly traded company, or possibly gin up some future merger with Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s SpinCo. My sense, particularly based on the signals that he sent Thursday, is that he\xe2\x80\x99s getting close to pulling the trigger here. \xe2\x80\x9cThis new structure will provide investors with better visibility to the strength of our streaming and studios business and will give us real strategic value and optionality into the future,\xe2\x80\x9d Zaz told the Wall Street analysts. When BofA\xe2\x80\x99s Jessica Reif Ehrlich asked about future strategic initiatives, Zaz replied that the company\xe2\x80\x99s new structure allowed it to be \xe2\x80\x9cbetter able to respond to this generational disruption\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9ccreate potential opportunities to unlock additional shareholder value, which we\xe2\x80\x99re focused on.\xe2\x80\x9d

Zaz tried to provide some hopium for the folks at CNN by citing the coming subscription and digital offerings being designed by Mark Thompson and Alex MacCallum , who \xe2\x80\x9cbuilt the digital business at The New York Times .\xe2\x80\x9d But that\xe2\x80\x99s still going to be a long putt. The WBD C.E.O. garnered perhaps the most attention for what he said about the company\xe2\x80\x99s sports agenda. (My partner John Ourand has done terrific reporting on this, of course.)


Our friend Rich Greenfield at LightShed Partners asked Zaz about the WBD sports strategy these days. He replied that his team is \xe2\x80\x9cdisciplined\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9copportunistic\xe2\x80\x9d when it comes to the sports rights opportunities and that WBD is \xe2\x80\x9cmoney good\xe2\x80\x9d on almost all of its current sports offerings: \xe2\x80\x9cWe don\xe2\x80\x99t need any more sports anywhere in the world in order to support our business.\xe2\x80\x9d Gunnar added that \xe2\x80\x9cseveral hundred million\xe2\x80\x9d dollars of expenses related to sports will be eliminated in 2026, along with some associated ad revenue, of course. \xe2\x80\x9cBut this should certainly be a few hundred million dollar improvement in 2026 over 2025,\xe2\x80\x9d he said.

A Zaz Ending?

Where WBD goes from here is anyone\xe2\x80\x99s guess, of course. Investor sentiment seems to be changing for the better. In the past six months, the stock is up 51 percent. But it\xe2\x80\x99s also down the aforementioned 55 percent since the company was formed nearly three years ago. Is the glass half empty or half full?


That\xe2\x80\x99s one of the most intriguing parlor games being played in Hollywood and on Wall Street these days. Zaz, of course, remains supremely optimistic. \xe2\x80\x9cWe really believe that the global players will be those that will really prosper in the years ahead, and ultimately be the largest sustainable growth media companies,\xe2\x80\x9d he told Barclays analyst Kannan Venkateshwar on the earnings call. \xe2\x80\x9cThere will probably be four, or five, or six, and our job every day is to fight to get a seat at that table.\xe2\x80\x9d The fourth quarter results, if they can be sustained, at least indicate that Zaz and WBD will likely be one of the \xe2\x80\x9cfour, five, or six.\xe2\x80\x9d

An insider-friendly tip sheet from Matthew Belloni, who spent 14 years in the trenches at The Hollywood Reporter and five before that as an entertainment lawyer. Subscribers also receive What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing+, a companion email focused on entertainment law, the streaming industry, and more.
